messages.content: Where do a person’s opinions on politics come from? make your response very short
A person's opinions on politics primarily stem from their upbringing, education, social environment, personal experiences, and media exposure.
What are three of the main influences on our political opinions? make your response very short
Family, media, and education.
What is the biggest thing the government can do to shape public opinions? Provide  2 examples. make your response very short
Implementing policies that significantly impact daily life. Examples: 1) Education reform to shape youth perspectives. 2) Media regulations to influence information flow.
More recently, what government issue has had an impact on public opinion? make your response very short
COVID-19 pandemic response.
What has caused Congress’ approval ratings to decline? make your response very short
Partisanship, gridlock, and perceived inefficiency.
Besides doing, or not doing their jobs, how does the government actively shape public opinion? make your response very short
Propaganda and media messaging.
Why is the President especially good at controlling the news cycle? make your response very short
High visibility and authority.
How did Theodore Roosevelt view the office of the presidency? make your response very short
As a "bully pulpit" for influence.
How did Franklin Roosevelt begin the trend of presidents speaking directly to the people? What did FDR use these conversations for? make your response very short
Through "Fireside Chats" on the radio, used for explaining policies and reassuring the public.
What was President Clinton known to have in regards to communicating with the public? make your response very short
Exceptional communication skills and charisma.
How have President’s Bush and Obama continued this trend? make your response very short
Bush and Obama utilized television and the internet, including social media, to communicate directly with the public.
What PR innovation has not worked well? Describe them.  make your response very short
Government-produced news segments which often face criticism for being propaganda rather than impartial news.
What historical explanation is behind why government efforts to create positive public opinion are less successful than we might think? make your response very short
Public skepticism towards government motives and messaging, rooted in historical instances of misinformation and propaganda.
What is public opinion highly reactive towards? make your response very short
Events, particularly crises and scandals.
Besides government, what other group can have a significant effect on the way the public views government and its policies? make your response very short
The media.
What can any group with a particular interest in legislation do? What will they need?  make your response very short
Lobby for or against the legislation. They will need resources and public support.
What is the NRA? What purpose does it serve?  make your response very short
The National Rifle Association (NRA) is a gun rights advocacy group, serving to protect and promote the Second Amendment rights.
How has the internet impacted public relations? make your response very short
Greatly increased speed and reach of communication.
Often, the group which is better at swaying public opinion has more what? make your response very short
Resources and strategy.
What was the Harry and Louise ad campaign? What message did it spread? make your response very short
A 1990s ad campaign opposing healthcare reform, spreading the message that proposed changes would limit patient choice.
How much did the Harry and Louise ad campaign cost?  make your response very short
Approximately $15 million.
What result did the Harry and Louise ad campaign achieve? make your response very short
It helped to turn public opinion against the 1993 Clinton health care plan, contributing to its failure.
How do public interest research groups with little money get their message across?  make your response very short
Through grassroots organizing and social media campaigns.
What are “think tanks”? make your response very short
Research organizations that develop and promote policy ideas.
What are some conservative (right) think tanks? make your response very short
The Heritage Foundation, American Enterprise Institute (AEI), Cato Institute.
What is a liberal (left) think tank?
Center for American Progress.
Why is the media the most powerful force in shaping public opinion?

The media is the primary source of information for the public, influencing perceptions, attitudes, and beliefs on a wide scale through news, entertainment, and social media platforms. Its pervasive reach and ability to frame discussions make it a powerful force in shaping public opinion.
What is the main way the media shapes public opinion? make your response very short
Framing issues and selectively presenting information.
What is priming? Provide an example. make your response very short
Priming is a media effect where exposure to a stimulus influences response to a subsequent stimulus. Example: News coverage on crime can prime viewers to prioritize safety in elections.
What is framing? Provide an example. make your response very short
Framing is presenting information in a way that influences perception. Example: Describing a protest as "a riot" versus "a peaceful demonstration" shapes public opinion on the event.
